question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
How old is the patient?
Is s/he conscious?
Is s/he breathing normally?
What was s/he exposed to? When did the exposure happen?
Was it a food, medication, insect sting, or something else?
How long ago did the symptoms start?
Is there any swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat?
Is s/he having difficulty breathing or swallowing?
Do you hear any wheezing or high-pitched breathing sounds?
Is there a rash or hives? How widespread?
Is the skin itchy?
Does s/he feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Has s/he vomited or does s/he have diarrhea?
Does s/he have abdominal cramping?
Has s/he had a severe allergic reaction before?
Does s/he have an EpiPen or epinephrine auto-injector?
Has the EpiPen been used? How many times?
Does s/he take any allergy medications like Benadryl?
Is the voice hoarse or different than normal?
Does s/he have asthma?
ECHO-level: Anaphylactic shock — unconscious, not breathing
DELTA-level: Throat swelling with severe difficulty breathing
DELTA-level: Tongue or lip swelling causing airway obstruction
DELTA-level: Anaphylaxis — hives with breathing difficulty and dizziness/hypotension
DELTA-level: Known severe allergy with rapid onset after exposure, altered consciousness
condition: known allergy AND rapid onset AND altered LOC

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Widespread hives with mild dyspnea, stable after EpiPen
condition: widespread urticaria AND mild dyspnea AND post-epi

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Facial swelling progressing but airway currently intact
condition: progressive facial swelling AND airway intact

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Insect sting with systemic reaction — hives beyond sting site
BRAVO-level: Moderate hives with no airway involvement, GI symptoms present
condition: moderate urticaria AND GI symptoms AND no airway

question_text: BRAVO-level: Allergic reaction with facial swelling, no breathing difficulty
ALPHA-level: Localized hives, no systemic symptoms, mild itching
condition: localized urticaria AND no systemic symptoms

question_text: ALPHA-level: Insect sting with local swelling only, no spreading
OMEGA-level: Mild rash, known allergy, oral antihistamine taken, symptoms improving
condition: mild rash AND improving AND antihistamine taken

question_text: If the patient has an EpiPen: remove the safety cap, press firmly into the outer thigh (through clothing is okay), hold for 10 seconds.
If a second EpiPen is available and symptoms are not improving after 5 minutes, use the second one.
Have the patient lie flat with legs elevated UNLESS they are having difficulty breathing — then sit them upright.
If the patient has Benadryl (diphenhydramine), give the appropriate dose by mouth if they can swallow safely.
Remove the stinger by scraping sideways with a credit card. Do NOT squeeze or use tweezers.
If the patient becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR immediately.
Watch for vomiting — turn the patient on their side if they start to vomit.
Stay on the line. Help is on the way.
If the symptoms get worse or s/he stops breathing, call us back immediately.
